title: Emergency Guide: Dog Swallowed an Alkaline Battery
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed an alkaline battery, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn the immediate risks and why you must contact a vet right away.
content_html:
TL;DR: Alkaline battery ingestion is a life-threatening emergency that causes rapid internal chemical burns; contact an emergency veterinarian or pet poison control immediately.

What happens if my dog swallows an alkaline battery?

An alkaline battery ingestion occurs when a dog chews or swallows a common household battery (such as AA, AAA, or D cells). Unlike other objects, batteries contain concentrated potassium or sodium hydroxide. When these chemicals leak, they create a corrosive reaction that can cause severe damage to internal tissues.

Is it an emergency if my dog eats a battery?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate intervention.
  • If the battery is punctured, the alkaline substance causes liquefactive necrosis, which rapidly dissolves living tissue.
  • Even if the battery remains intact, it poses a significant risk of internal chemical burns or a physical obstruction in the gastrointestinal tract.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control center immediately.

How can a photo of the battery help with veterinary triage?

  • If you have the packaging or a matching battery, take a clear photo to help the veterinary team identify the specific size, chemistry, and brand.
  • Knowing exactly what was swallowed allows the vet to determine the risk of heavy metal toxicity.
  • The image helps the vet decide if the battery is small enough to pass or large enough to require surgical removal.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of alkaline batteries poses a risk of gastrointestinal tract corrosive injury and foreign body obstruction in dogs. Alkaline batteries contain potassium hydroxide or sodium hydroxide that causes liquefactive necrosis on contact, resulting in burns. Lithium disk batteries may lodge in the esophagus and generate a current against the esophageal walls, resulting in circular ulcers. Radiography is performed to determine battery location, and batteries that do not pass through the pylorus within 48 hours of ingestion may require surgical or endoscopic removal. If the battery casing is punctured, surgical removal is indicated to prevent gastric or intestinal ulceration due to leakage of alkaline gel. For intact batteries, emesis may be induced unless puncture is suspected. For disk batteries, administer 20 mL boluses of tap water every 15 minutes to decrease the severity and delay esophageal ulceration.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3003)

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. You should never induce vomiting if a battery was swallowed. If the battery is punctured or leaking, the corrosive liquid will cause even more severe chemical burns to the esophagus on the way back up.

faqs.question: What are the signs of battery poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive drooling, oral ulcers (white or grey patches in the mouth), refusal to eat, abdominal pain, and black, tarry stools.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog anything to eat or drink?
faqs.answer: Do not give your dog food, water, or milk until you have spoken with a veterinarian, as this may interfere with necessary diagnostic imaging or emergency surgery.
